summary:
Ibn Battuta's travels (1325–1354 AD) constituted the most extensive journey by any individual in the premodern world, covering an estimated 117,000 kilometers — roughly three times the distance of [[event:2230|Marco Polo's]] travels — across the entirety of the Islamic world and far beyond, from Morocco to China, from Mali to the Maldives, from Anatolia to the Central Asian steppe. A Moroccan jurist from Tangier, Ibn Battuta set out initially on pilgrimage to Mecca and simply never stopped traveling for twenty-nine years, driven by insatiable curiosity and the network of Islamic institutions (mosques, madrasas, hospices) that provided hospitality to traveling scholars across the Dar al-Islam.

Ibn Battuta's account, the Rihla ('Journey'), dictated to the scholar Ibn Juzayy at the command of the Marinid sultan of Morocco upon his return, is one of the most valuable historical sources for 14th-century world geography, sociology, and political life. His descriptions of [[event:2257|Mali under Mansa Musa's]] successors provide the most detailed account of that empire's institutions; his visit to the [[event:100|Yuan Dynasty China]] describes a world being reorganized after [[event:91|Mongol]] conquest; his account of the [[event:2305|Golden Horde]] steppe society gives an extraordinary window into nomadic life. Unlike Marco Polo, whose account was doubted by contemporaries, Ibn Battuta was a trained legal scholar whose observations aimed at practical accuracy — though he undoubtedly exaggerated some details and borrowed others from earlier travelers. His travels demonstrate the extraordinary connectivity of the 14th-century Islamic world, where a scholar's credentials could secure hospitality, employment, and even high office from Morocco to China.
